character_dossiers.name: Captain Trelawney
biographies: Mentioned in the context of the captain's papers.
A seafaring man who frequents the Admiral Benbow Inn, avoids other seafarers, and is feared for his stories and drunken outbursts. He wears patched clothing and keeps a sea-chest.
A man at the inn with a blue nose, startled by Black Dog, and later reeling from rum. He is described as having a commanding presence.
Suffered a severe injury and stroke, demanded rum, and referenced a mysterious 'Black Dog'.
A weakened man recovering from illness, involved in a conflict with Black Dog and a secret about the black spot. Interacts with Jim, who cares for him.
A sick old buccaneer who sits with a drawn cutlass and dies from apoplexy.
A deceased pirate whose body is found in the inn, with a key and the Black Spot discovered on his person.
A figure associated with the stolen coins and the bag containing the money.
dead, whose body was searched
Mentioned in context of the stolen money-bag and the plot involving the lugger.
Mentioned in context of a thimble found in his pocket.
A former presence at the inn who caused discomfort and is no longer there.
Described as the captain of the ship, involved in discussions with the crew.
Captain of the ship, expresses dissatisfaction with the voyage and crew.
The ship's captain who ordered Mr. Arrow below and confirmed his disappearance.
A captain with a terse demeanor, involved in disputes with the squire and discussions about the crew.
Leader of the crew who navigates the ship.
A naval officer leading a ship's crew, involved in planning and decision-making with Dr. Livesey and the squire.
Participated in a council discussing mutiny, relied on Silver to manage the situation.
Addressed the crew, ordered them ashore, and arranged for the party to be split. Noted as the leader but Silver took over the arrangement.
A ship captain who was displeased with his crew's decision to land on the island.
Leader of the crew, involved in planning and decision-making during the crisis. Reacts to the situation with urgency and authority.
Commander of the ship, orders crew to follow him during critical moments.
Crew member skilled in marksmanship and strategic planning.
Led the boat crew during a perilous landing, ordered actions to avoid gunfire, and retrieved supplies from the wrecked boat.
Identified as the dead shot, exchanged guns with the captain, and remained calm during the conflict.
Leader of the group ashore, making decisions about supplies and the mutineers' actions.
Leader of the group, giving orders to the crew, involved in a confrontation with Long John.
A captain addressed by the mutineers during the negotiation.
Leader of a group refusing to surrender the chart, threatening Silver.
Directed defense preparations, reprimanded Gray for negligence, assigned positions to crew members, and strategized against the attack.
Assigned to the north side with Gray, acknowledged as the best shot, and participated in defensive coordination.
Led defensive efforts at the log-house, sustained injuries, and issued orders during the battle.
Provided medical care, assessed casualties, and communicated with the group post-battle.
Suffered grievous wounds but was expected to recover, with his shoulder-blade broken and lung damaged. Required rest and care.
Survived the mutiny with severe wounds but was expected to recover.
Captain of the ship HISPANIOLA, referenced in the context of being opposed by the narrator's plan to cut the ship adrift.
Mentioned by the narrator in a hypothetical comparison to a captain's command of crew behavior.
